questions.question:
Consider the following statements regarding the slow start phase of the TCP congestion control algorithm. Note that cwnd stands for the TCP congestion window and MSS denotes the Maximum Segment Size:

i. The cwnd increases by MSS on every successful acknowledgment
ii. The cwnd approximately doubles on every successful acknowledgment
iii. The cwnd increases by 1 MSS every round trip time
iv. The cwnd approximately doubles every round trip time

Which one of the following is correct?
A. Only (i) and (iii) are true
B. Only (i) and (ii) are true
C. Only (ii) and (iv) are true
D. Only (i) and (iv) are true
questions.to_find: Which statements about TCP slow start phase are correct?

understand.plain: TCP slow start is the initial phase of TCP congestion control where the sender starts with a small congestion window (cwnd) and grows it rapidly. In this phase, cwnd increases by 1 MSS for every ACK received. This causes the window to double each round trip time (RTT), giving exponential growth. The question asks which statements correctly describe this behavior.

keywords.explain: The sender-side limit on how much unacknowledged data can be in flight; in slow start it grows exponentially.
keywords.example: If cwnd = 4 MSS and 4 ACKs arrive, cwnd becomes 8 MSS — doubled.

keywords.explain: The largest TCP segment payload that can be sent in one packet, typically 1460 bytes on Ethernet.
keywords.example: If MSS = 1000 bytes and cwnd = 3 MSS, sender can send 3000 bytes before waiting for ACK.

keywords.explain: TCP congestion control phase where cwnd grows by 1 MSS per ACK, doubling each RTT until ssthresh is reached.

stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='260' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>+1 MSS per ACK => doubles per RTT</text><text x='10' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag' transform='rotate(-90,10,140)'>cwnd (MSS)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Graph showing TCP slow start cwnd growth: starts at 1 MSS and doubles each RTT (1, 2, 4, 8, 16 MSS), demonstrating exponential growth.

given.aim: Identify which statements correctly describe TCP slow start cwnd behavior.

terms.term: Statement (i): cwnd increases by MSS on every successful ACK
terms.meaning: For each ACK received during slow start, cwnd grows by exactly 1 MSS.
terms.example: cwnd = 4 MSS; receive 1 ACK -> cwnd = 5 MSS.
terms.connects: This is the fundamental rule of slow start — TRUE.

terms.term: Statement (ii): cwnd approximately doubles on every successful acknowledgment
terms.meaning: cwnd doubles with each individual ACK — this would be extremely fast growth.
terms.example: cwnd = 4 MSS; after 1 ACK -> cwnd = 8 MSS — this is WRONG.
terms.connects: Doubling happens per RTT (when all cwnd ACKs arrive), not per single ACK — FALSE.

terms.term: Statement (iv): cwnd approximately doubles every round trip time
terms.meaning: In one RTT, cwnd many ACKs arrive equal to current cwnd, each adding 1 MSS, so cwnd doubles.
terms.example: cwnd = 4 MSS; 4 ACKs arrive in 1 RTT; cwnd becomes 4+4 = 8 MSS.
terms.connects: This correctly describes the exponential growth of slow start — TRUE.

given.plan: Analyze each statement against the TCP slow start rule: cwnd += 1 MSS per ACK received. Determine which statements are true and match the answer option.

steps.title: Analyze TCP Slow Start Rule
steps.formula_raw: cwnd(new) = cwnd(old) + MSS for each ACK received
steps.apply: In the slow start phase, for every ACK that arrives, the sender increases cwnd by 1 MSS. This is the defining rule of slow start. Statement (i) says 'cwnd increases by MSS on every successful acknowledgment' — this is TRUE.
steps.note: Statement (iii) says cwnd increases by 1 MSS every RTT — this describes congestion avoidance (AIMD), NOT slow start. So (iii) is FALSE.

steps.title: Compute Growth per RTT to Evaluate Statements (ii) and (iv)
steps.formula_raw: If cwnd = W at start of RTT, then W ACKs arrive -> cwnd = W + W = 2W
steps.apply: If cwnd = W MSS at the start of an RTT, the sender sends W segments. When all W ACKs return, each increments cwnd by 1 MSS, so cwnd grows by W MSS total, reaching 2W. Thus cwnd DOUBLES each RTT — statement (iv) is TRUE. Statement (ii) says cwnd doubles on every SINGLE acknowledgment, which would mean cwnd = 1 -> 2 -> 4 after just 2 ACKs. That is wrong — doubling is per RTT, not per ACK. So (ii) is FALSE.
steps.note: The distinction between 'per ACK' and 'per RTT' is critical: +1 MSS per ACK leads to x2 per RTT when cwnd ACKs arrive in one RTT.

steps.title: Match to Answer Options
steps.apply: TRUE statements: (i) and (iv). FALSE statements: (ii) and (iii). The option 'Only (i) and (iv) are true' corresponds to option D.

questions.formula_note: TCP Slow Start: cwnd += 1 MSS per ACK, leading to doubling of cwnd per RTT (exponential growth) until ssthresh is reached.

questions.question:
On a TCP connection, current congestion window size is Congestion Window = 4 KB. The window size advertised by the receiver is Advertise Window = 6 KB. The last byte sent by the sender is LastByteSent = 10240 and the last byte acknowledged by the receiver is LastByteAcked = 8192. The current window size at the sender is:
A. 2048 bytes
B. 4096 bytes
C. 6144 bytes
D. 8192 bytes
questions.to_find: Current effective sender window size (how many more bytes can the sender transmit right now).

understand.plain: TCP sender has two window limits: the congestion window (cwnd, set by congestion control) and the advertise window (rwnd, set by the receiver's buffer). The effective send window is the minimum of these two. But the question asks how many bytes the sender can STILL send right now, considering bytes already sent but not yet acknowledged.

keywords.explain: The actual number of bytes the sender can send at any moment = min(cwnd, rwnd) - (LastByteSent - LastByteAcked).
keywords.example: If min(cwnd,rwnd)=4KB and 2KB are already in-flight, effective window = 2KB.

keywords.explain: Bytes sent but not yet acknowledged: LastByteSent - LastByteAcked.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 540 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='540' height='200' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='270' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>TCP Sender Window Analysis</text><rect x='30' y='60' width='120' height='40' rx='4' fill='#B8E6CC' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='90' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>ACKed</text><text x='90' y='102' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>0 - 8192</text><rect x='150' y='60' width='90' height='40' rx='4' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='195' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>In-Flight</text><text x='195' y='98' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>2048 B</text><rect x='240' y='60' width='90' height='40' rx='4' fill='#FFE5CC' stroke='#E07030' stroke-width='2'/><text x='285' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>Allowed</text><text x='285' y='98' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>(2048 B)</text><rect x='330' y='60' width='170' height='40' rx='4' fill='#F1EFE8' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='415' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='mut tag'>Not Allowed</text><line x1='30' y1='140' x2='510' y2='140' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1'/><text x='150' y='158' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>8192</text><text x='240' y='158' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>10240</text><text x='330' y='158' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>12288</text><text x='90' y='175' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>LastByteAcked</text><text x='195' y='175' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>LastByteSent</text><text x='310' y='175' text-anchor='middle' class='mut sub'>min(cwnd,rwnd) limit</text><text x='130' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>cwnd=4KB, rwnd=6KB => min=4KB=4096B</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: TCP sender window diagram showing ACKed bytes (0-8192), in-flight bytes (8192-10240, 2048 bytes), and remaining allowed bytes (10240-12288, 2048 bytes). The effective window is min(cwnd=4KB, rwnd=6KB) = 4KB, minus in-flight 2048 bytes = 2048 bytes remaining.

given.aim: Find the number of bytes the sender can still transmit right now.

terms.meaning: The congestion control limit on unacknowledged data.
terms.example: Sender cannot have more than 4096 bytes unacknowledged due to congestion control.
terms.connects: One of two upper bounds on unacknowledged data.

terms.meaning: The receiver's buffer space limit communicated to the sender.
terms.example: Receiver can buffer up to 6144 more bytes.
terms.connects: Second upper bound; effective window = min(cwnd, rwnd).

terms.meaning: 10240 is the sequence number of the last byte sent; 8192 is the last acknowledged byte.
terms.example: Bytes 8193 to 10240 are sent but not yet ACKed (in-flight).
terms.connects: In-flight data = 10240 - 8192 = 2048 bytes, which reduces the available window.

given.plan: Compute effective window = min(cwnd, rwnd). Then subtract in-flight bytes (LastByteSent - LastByteAcked) to find how many more bytes can be sent now.

steps.title: Compute Effective Window Size
steps.apply: cwnd = 4 KB = 4096 bytes, rwnd = 6 KB = 6144 bytes. min(4096, 6144) = 4096 bytes.
steps.note: The sender is constrained by the smaller of congestion window and receiver's advertised window.

steps.title: Compute Bytes Already In-Flight
steps.apply: InFlight = 10240 - 8192 = 2048 bytes. These bytes have been sent but not yet acknowledged, so they count against the window.
steps.note: In-flight bytes consume part of the effective window even though they haven't been ACKed yet.

steps.title: Compute Remaining Window (Bytes Sender Can Still Send)
steps.apply: CanSend = 4096 - 2048 = 2048 bytes. The sender can transmit 2048 more bytes right now.
steps.note: This is the actual 'current window size at the sender' — how many new bytes it may send immediately.

questions.formula_note: TCP sender window: CanSend = min(cwnd, rwnd) - (LastByteSent - LastByteAcked); balances congestion control and receiver buffer limits.

questions.question:
Consider a network with 6 routers R1 to R6 connected with links having weights as shown in the following diagram.

(Network topology: R1 connects to R2 (weight 2), R1 connects to R3 (weight 7), R2 connects to R4 (weight 3), R3 connects to R4 (weight 5), R3 connects to R5 (weight 1), R4 connects to R5 (weight 4), R4 connects to R6 (weight 3), R5 connects to R6 (weight 1))

All the routers use the distance vector based routing algorithm to update their routing tables. Each router starts with its routing table initialized to contain an entry for each neighbor with the weight of the respective connecting link. After all the routing tables are stabilized, how many links will never be used for carrying any data?

A. 4
B. 3
C. 2
D. 1
questions.to_find: Number of links that are never used in optimal routing after distance vector stabilization.

understand.plain: Distance vector routing converges to the shortest paths between all pairs. Once routing tables stabilize, each router forwards packets on the shortest path. A link is 'never used' if it is NOT part of any shortest path between any source-destination pair. We need to find the shortest path tree and count unused links.

keywords.explain: Each router maintains a table with distances to all destinations and updates it by exchanging tables with neighbors (Bellman-Ford).
keywords.example: R1 learns shortest path to R6 by comparing all paths through its neighbors.

keywords.explain: After convergence, traffic follows the minimum-weight paths; links not on any shortest path carry no data.
keywords.example: If R1-R3 direct link (weight 7) is longer than R1-R2-R4-R3, the R1-R3 link may not be used.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 500 320'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='500' height='320' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='25' text-anchor='middle' class='ink lbl'>Network Topology (R1-R6)</text><line x1='100' y1='160' x2='200' y2='90' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='140' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>2</text><line x1='100' y1='160' x2='200' y2='240' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-dasharray='8,4'/><text x='140' y='210' text-anchor='middle' class='no tag'>7</text><line x1='200' y1='90' x2='310' y2='90' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='255' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>3</text><line x1='200' y1='240' x2='310' y2='90' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='248' y='178' text-anchor='middle' class='mut tag'>5</text><line x1='200' y1='240' x2='310' y2='240' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='255' y='232' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>1</text><line x1='310' y1='90' x2='310' y2='240' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='320' y='172' text-anchor='middle' class='mut tag'>4</text><line x1='310' y1='90' x2='400' y2='160' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='362' y='112' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>3</text><line x1='310' y1='240' x2='400' y2='160' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='362' y='215' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>1</text><circle cx='100' cy='160' r='20' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='100' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>R1</text><circle cx='200' cy='90' r='20' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='200' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>R2</text><circle cx='200' cy='240' r='20' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='200' y='245' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>R3</text><circle cx='310' cy='90' r='20' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='310' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>R4</text><circle cx='310' cy='240' r='20' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='310' y='245' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>R5</text><circle cx='400' cy='160' r='20' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='400' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='ink tag'>R6</text><text x='250' y='290' text-anchor='middle' class='no sub'>Red dashed = never used (R1-R3, weight 7)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Network with 6 routers: R1-R2 (2), R1-R3 (7), R2-R4 (3), R3-R4 (5), R3-R5 (1), R4-R5 (4), R4-R6 (3), R5-R6 (1). Green links are on shortest paths; the dashed red link R1-R3 (weight 7) is never used.

given.aim: Find how many links are never used for data forwarding after routing stabilization.

terms.connects: Used to compute shortest paths between all router pairs.

terms.meaning: R1 to R2: 2 (direct), R1 to R3: 7 vs 2+3+5=10 vs 2+3+4+1=10 vs 2+3+1=6... need full computation.
terms.example: R1->R3: direct=7, via R2->R4->R3=2+3+5=10, via R2->R4->R5->R3=2+3+4+1=10. So direct (7) is shorter.
terms.connects: Determines which links are on the shortest path tree.

terms.meaning: This link connects R4 and R5 with weight 4.
terms.example: R4 to R5 direct = 4, but via R6: R4->R6->... no. Via R3: R4->R3->R5 = 5+1=6 (longer). So R4-R5 direct is shortest for R4<->R5.
terms.connects: Need to check if R4-R5 is on any shortest path between any pair.

given.plan: Compute all-pairs shortest paths using Dijkstra or inspection. A link (u,v) is used if it lies on the shortest path between some pair of routers. Count links not on any shortest path.

steps.title: Compute Shortest Paths from Each Router (Dijkstra)
steps.formula_raw: d[v] = min over neighbors u of (d[u] + w(u,v))
steps.note: Both R1-R3 (direct, weight 7) and R1-R2-R4-R6 (weight 8) paths are used. R1-R3 link IS on a shortest path (R1 to R3, and R1 to R5).

steps.title: Check Each Link for Usage in ANY Shortest Path
steps.formula_raw: Link (u,v) used if: d(s,u) + w(u,v) + d(v,t) = d(s,t) for some s,t
steps.apply:
Links and usage:
- R1-R2 (2): Used for R1->R2, R1->R4, R1->R6. USED.
- R1-R3 (7): Used for R1->R3 (d=7), R1->R5 (via R1-R3-R5, d=8). USED.
- R2-R4 (3): Used for R1->R4, R1->R6, R2->R4, R2->R6. USED.
- R3-R4 (5): Check: d(R3,R4)=5 direct vs via R3-R5-R4=1+4=5 (tie!). So R3-R4 may or may not be on a shortest path. Is it on any pair? R1->R4 via R1-R3-R4 = 7+5=12 (not shortest, d=5). R2->R3 via R2-R4-R3=3+5=8 vs R2-R1-R3=2+7=9. So R2->R3 shortest is 8 via R2-R4-R3. USED.
- R3-R5 (1): Used for R1->R5, R3->R5, etc. USED.
- R4-R5 (4): d(R4,R5) direct=4. Via R4-R3-R5=5+1=6 (longer). Via R4-R6-R5: d(R4,R6)=3, d(R6,R5)=1, so R4-R6-R5=3+1=4 (TIE with direct!). So for R4-R5 pair, direct and via R6 are equal. Is R4-R5 used for any other pair? R2->R5: d=2+3+4=9 via R2-R4-R5, vs R2-R1-R3-R5=2+7+1=10, vs R2-R4-R6-R5=2+3+3+1=9 (tie). R1->R5 via R1-R3-R5=8, vs R1-R2-R4-R5=2+3+4=9 (longer). So R4-R5 direct is NOT on R1->R5 shortest. For R4<->R5: both direct and via R6 cost 4. If via-R6 is preferred, R4-R5 direct is NEVER USED.
- R4-R6 (3): Used for R1->R6 (R1-R2-R4-R6=8), R4->R6. USED.
- R5-R6 (1): Used for R5->R6, R3->R6 (R3-R5-R6=2), R1->R6 via R1-R3-R5-R6=9 (not shortest since R1-R2-R4-R6=8 is better). R4->R6 via R4-R5-R6=4+1=5 vs direct R4-R6=3 (direct is shorter). So R5-R6 is used for R5->R6, R3->R5-R6, etc. USED.

Conclusion: R4-R5 (weight 4) is potentially never used, since d(R4,R5)=4 can be achieved via R4-R6-R5=3+1=4 as well, and for all other pairs there is an equally short or shorter path not using R4-R5.
steps.note: The key insight: R4-R5 direct (weight 4) = R4-R6-R5 (weight 3+1=4). Since the path via R6 achieves the same cost, and for distant pairs R4-R5 direct is never strictly needed, R4-R5 is never the unique shortest path for any pair. Answer: 1 link never used.

questions.formula_note: Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm; a link is 'never used' if it is not on any shortest path between any source-destination pair in the stabilized distance-vector routing table.
